questions.question:
A transparent square sheet shown above is folded along the dotted line (a diagonal). The folded sheet will look like ________________.

A. A square with a triangular flap on the top-right, fold crease running bottom-left to top-right
B. A triangle with a smaller triangle overlapping inside, forming a symmetric diamond outline
C. A right-angled triangle showing two layers aligned perfectly (the transparent sheet folds one half exactly over the other)
D. A square with a triangle cut-out in the lower-left corner
questions.answer: C. A right-angled triangle showing two layers aligned perfectly (the transparent sheet folds one half exactly over the other)
questions.to_find: The appearance of a transparent square sheet after folding along its diagonal

understand.plain: A transparent square sheet is folded along one of its diagonals (a dotted line from one corner to the opposite corner). Because the sheet is transparent, both layers are visible. Folding along a diagonal brings one triangular half exactly on top of the other, producing a right-angled isosceles triangle. Since the material is transparent, both layers overlap and you see a single filled right-angled triangle with no stray edges.

keywords.explain: A sheet through which both layers are visible when overlapped, unlike opaque paper where the back side is hidden.
keywords.example: Tracing paper or overhead projector film folded in half shows both halves at once.

keywords.explain: Folding along the line connecting two opposite corners of a square, creating two congruent right-angled isosceles triangles.
keywords.example: A 4x4 square folded along the diagonal gives a right triangle with legs of length 4.

keywords.explain: Both triangular halves are identical in size and shape, so when one is folded onto the other they coincide perfectly.
keywords.example: The fold crease lies exactly at the hypotenuse; top and bottom edges match corner to corner.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 180' width='420' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='180' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><rect x='20' y='30' width='100' height='100' class='hi' fill-opacity='0.3'/><line x1='20' y1='130' x2='120' y2='30' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-dasharray='6,4'/><text x='70' y='155' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Before fold</text><text x='200' y='90' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>→</text><polygon points='240,130 340,130 340,30' class='hi' fill-opacity='0.5'/><text x='290' y='155' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>After fold (C)</text><text x='290' y='85' class='tag mut' text-anchor='middle'>two layers</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Left: a square with a dashed diagonal; right: the resulting right-angled triangle after folding along that diagonal.

given.aim: Identify the shape seen after folding a transparent square along its diagonal

terms.meaning: A flat, equal-sided quadrilateral serving as the starting shape.
terms.example: A standard square with corners at (0,0), (1,0), (1,1), (0,1).
terms.connects: Its diagonal is the fold line that divides it into two congruent right triangles.

terms.meaning: The crease along which one half of the square is lifted and placed on the other half.
terms.connects: After folding, the hypotenuse of the resulting triangle corresponds to this diagonal.

terms.meaning: Because the sheet is transparent both layers are visible after folding, making the outline a single solid right triangle.
terms.example: Two congruent transparent triangles stacked look like one opaque triangle.
terms.connects: This rules out options showing separate or displaced shapes.

given.plan: Visualise the square, draw its diagonal, fold the top-left triangle down onto the bottom-right triangle, and observe the resulting outline — a right-angled isosceles triangle with both layers perfectly aligned.

steps.title: Identify the fold line
steps.apply: The dotted line on the square runs from one corner to the diagonally opposite corner. This is the main diagonal. Folding along it splits the square into two congruent right-angled isosceles triangles.
steps.note: The fold crease becomes the hypotenuse of the resulting triangle.

steps.title: Perform the fold
steps.apply: Lift one triangular half and place it exactly over the other. Because both halves are congruent, all edges and corners align perfectly. The result is a right-angled isosceles triangle of half the original area.
steps.note: Transparent material means both layers are visible but appear as a single filled triangle.

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: Option C shows a right-angled triangle with both layers coinciding and no displaced or misaligned edges. This matches the folded result. Options A, B, D show shapes inconsistent with a clean diagonal fold of a square.

solution.result: C — the folded sheet looks like a right-angled isosceles triangle (the two halves overlap perfectly).

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula is required; the solution relies on visualising a diagonal fold of a square.

questions.question:
Consider a square sheet of side 1 unit. The sheet is first folded along the main diagonal. This is followed by a fold along its line of symmetry. The resulting folded shape is again folded along its line of symmetry. The area of each face of the final folded shape, in square units, equal to _________.

A. 1/4
B. 1/8
C. 1/16
D. 1/2
questions.to_find: Area of each face of the final folded shape after three successive folds

understand.plain: We start with a unit square of area 1. Each fold along a line of symmetry halves the visible area (one half is placed on top of the other). After three such folds the area of each face is 1 divided by 2^3 = 1/8. The number of layers doubles with every fold, but the question asks for the area of one face, not the total area.

keywords.explain: Folding along a line of symmetry brings one half of the shape exactly onto the other half, halving the surface area of the visible face.
keywords.example: A 1x1 square folded along its horizontal midline produces a 1x0.5 rectangle of area 1/2.

keywords.explain: The first fold is along the diagonal of the square, producing a right-angled isosceles triangle with face area 1/2.
keywords.example: 1x1 square, area 1; fold along diagonal gives a triangle of face area 1/2.

keywords.explain: Each fold halves the face area: fold 1 gives 1/2, fold 2 gives 1/4, fold 3 gives 1/8.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 160' width='520' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:22px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:16px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:13px;}.tag{font-size:13px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='160' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><rect x='10' y='30' width='80' height='80' class='hi' fill-opacity='0.3'/><text x='50' y='125' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Square</text><text x='50' y='140' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>area=1</text><text x='105' y='78' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>→</text><polygon points='130,110 210,110 210,30' class='hi' fill-opacity='0.4'/><text x='170' y='125' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Fold 1</text><text x='170' y='140' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>area=1/2</text><text x='225' y='78' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>→</text><polygon points='245,110 285,110 285,70 265,70' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><polygon points='245,110 285,70 245,70' class='hi' fill-opacity='0.45'/><text x='265' y='125' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Fold 2</text><text x='265' y='140' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>area=1/4</text><text x='305' y='78' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>→</text><polygon points='320,110 360,90 340,70 320,90' class='hi' fill-opacity='0.55'/><text x='340' y='125' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Fold 3</text><text x='340' y='140' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>area=1/8</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A sequence of four shapes: unit square (area 1), right triangle (area 1/2), smaller triangle (area 1/4), smallest triangle (area 1/8) after three folds.

given.aim: Find the face area of the final shape after 3 consecutive symmetry folds starting from a unit square

terms.meaning: A square with each side equal to 1 unit; initial area = 1 sq unit.
terms.connects: The starting area from which each fold reduces the face area.

terms.meaning: Folding along the diagonal of the unit square gives a right isosceles triangle with legs 1 and hypotenuse sqrt(2). Face area = 1/2.
terms.connects: Sets up subsequent folds along lines of symmetry of the triangle.

terms.term: Fold 2 and Fold 3 — lines of symmetry
terms.meaning: Each fold along the line of symmetry of the current shape halves its face area.
terms.example: After fold 2: area = 1/4. After fold 3: area = 1/8.
terms.connects: Three folds total mean the area is divided by 2^3 = 8.

given.plan: Apply the area-halving rule three times: area after n folds = 1 / 2^n. For n=3, area = 1/8.

steps.title: Initial area
steps.apply: The square sheet has side 1 unit, so its initial area is 1 sq unit.
steps.note: This is the area before any fold.

steps.title: Fold 1: along the main diagonal
steps.apply: Folding along the main diagonal of a square halves the face area. The resulting shape is a right-angled isosceles triangle. Area = 1/2 sq unit.
steps.note: The fold crease is the hypotenuse of the triangle.

steps.title: Fold 2: along the line of symmetry of the triangle
steps.apply: The right isosceles triangle is folded along its altitude from the right-angle vertex (its line of symmetry), producing a smaller right isosceles triangle. Area = 1/4 sq unit.
steps.note: Both halves are congruent and overlap perfectly.

steps.title: Fold 3: along the line of symmetry of the current shape
steps.apply: Folding again along the line of symmetry of the triangle halves the face area once more. Final face area = 1/8 sq unit.

solution.result: The area of each face of the final folded shape is 1/8 sq unit. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: No registry formula is needed; the key relationship is that each fold along a line of symmetry halves the face area: Area_n = 1 / 2^n.

questions.question:
A planar rectangular paper has two V-shaped pieces attached as shown below. This piece of paper is folded to make the following closed three-dimensional object (a prism-like shape with V-shaped cross-sections). The number of folds required to form the above object is

A. 9
B. 7
C. 11
D. 8
questions.to_find: The number of folds required to form the described closed three-dimensional object from the flat rectangular paper with V-shaped attachments

understand.plain: A flat net (rectangle plus two V-shaped tabs) is folded along crease lines to form a closed 3D prism-like object. Each crease in the net corresponds to exactly one fold. Counting the crease lines in the net gives the total number of folds. For the given net, there are 9 fold lines required to close the shape completely.

keywords.explain: A flat (2D) shape that can be folded along marked crease lines to form a 3D solid.
keywords.example: The net of a cube has 6 faces connected by 5 fold lines (plus tabs).

keywords.explain: A trapezoidal or triangular attachment on the rectangular paper that folds to form the angled faces of the V-cross-section prism.
keywords.example: Each V piece requires 2 folds (one on each arm of the V) and 1 fold to close the tab.

keywords.explain: The total number of distinct crease lines along which the flat net must be folded to produce the closed 3D shape.
keywords.example: 9 folds in this net: 3 for the rectangular body + 3 per V-tab x 2 tabs = 3 + 6 = 9.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 200' width='480' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:22px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:16px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:13px;}.tag{font-size:13px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='200' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><rect x='80' y='70' width='200' height='60' class='bg'/><polygon points='80,70 50,40 80,100' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='80,100 50,130 80,130' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='280,70 310,40 280,100' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='280,100 310,130 280,130' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='130' y1='70' x2='130' y2='130' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><line x1='180' y1='70' x2='180' y2='130' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><line x1='230' y1='70' x2='230' y2='130' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><text x='180' y='165' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Flat net (dashed = fold lines)</text><text x='360' y='100' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='start'>9 folds total</text><text x='360' y='118' class='sub mut' text-anchor='start'>3 body + 3+3 tabs</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Flat rectangular net with two V-shaped tabs on each short side; dashed lines indicate fold creases leading to 9 total folds.

given.aim: Count the number of fold lines in the flat net required to close it into the given 3D object

terms.meaning: The central rectangular portion of the net that forms the four long faces of the prism.
terms.example: A rectangular strip divided into 4 equal panels by 3 vertical fold lines contributes 3 folds.
terms.connects: These 3 folds close the rectangle into the tubular body of the prism.

terms.meaning: The V-piece on the left end of the rectangle; each arm of the V requires one fold, and closing the tip requires one more — 3 folds per V-piece.
terms.example: Left V: fold at junction with rectangle (1) + fold along left arm (2) + fold along right arm (3).
terms.connects: Provides the angled end cap on one side of the 3D object.

terms.meaning: Mirror image of the left V-piece; also requires 3 folds.
terms.example: Right V: fold at junction (1) + fold along left arm (2) + fold along right arm (3).
terms.connects: Closes the other end of the prism to make the object fully enclosed.

given.plan: Count fold lines in the net: 3 along the rectangular body + 3 for the left V-tab + 3 for the right V-tab = 9 total folds.

steps.title: Count folds in the rectangular body
steps.apply: The central rectangle is divided into 4 face-panels by 3 vertical crease lines. Folding along these 3 lines bends the strip into a closed tube (the body of the prism). Body folds = 3.
steps.note: The 4th edge is where the two ends of the strip meet to close the tube — this junction is a fold/tab, counted here.

steps.title: Count folds in the left V-shaped tab
steps.apply: The V-piece on the left consists of 2 angled arms and a connecting base. It requires 3 fold lines: one at the attachment edge (connecting it to the rectangle), and one along each arm of the V. Left tab folds = 3.
steps.note: These 3 folds form the triangular/V cross-section end cap.

steps.title: Count folds in the right V-shaped tab
steps.apply: The right V-piece is symmetric to the left and also requires 3 fold lines. Right tab folds = 3.
steps.note: Closing both V-tabs seals the 3D object.

solution.result: The number of folds required is 9. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula from the registry is needed; the answer follows from systematically counting the crease lines in the flat net of the 3D object.
